us-politics.headline: US Jets Downed Over Iran as Trump Threatens Escalation
us-politics.body:
American military aircraft have been shot down by Iranian fire for the first time in over two decades, marking a sharp escalation in the ongoing US-Iran conflict. Trump said the war is "near completion" while simultaneously raising new threats against Tehran, a combination that analysts across the spectrum are struggling to reconcile.

Conservative commentators frame the moment as a test of presidential resolve, arguing Trump must project strength to force a settlement. Critics on the left and centre warn that downed jets and hardening rhetoric signal a conflict drifting beyond its original scope. The administration separately arrested relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani living in the US after Secretary of State Rubio revoked their green cards, a move supporters call leverage and opponents call collective punishment.

sources.title: Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’
TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years
The US can beat Iran at its own game, and without boots on the ground
ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards
Rubio revokes green cards of 2 Soleimani relatives amid Iran conflict

us-politics.headline: Judge Blocks Trump Push to Police College Admissions on Race
us-politics.body:
A federal judge has halted a Trump administration effort that would have required colleges to certify they are not considering race in admissions decisions. The administration framed the requirement as enforcing the Supreme Court's 2023 ruling against affirmative action. Critics argued it amounted to a surveillance mechanism that went well beyond what the court ordered.

The ruling is a setback for the administration's broader campaign to reshape higher education policy through executive pressure. Supporters of the move say universities have quietly continued race-conscious practices and that federal oversight is the only enforcement tool available. Opponents say the certification demand was vague, legally overreaching, and designed to intimidate institutions rather than clarify the law.

sources.title: Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions
Judge halts Trump effort requiring colleges to show they don't consider race in admissions

us-politics.headline: ICE Clash, Senate Dispute, and a Louisiana Parade Crash
us-politics.body:
Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen accused ICE of causing a crash while pursuing an undocumented immigrant. DHS pushed back sharply, saying the individual fled agents and caused the collision, placing responsibility on the suspect rather than the agency. The dispute reflects a broader pattern of competing narratives around immigration enforcement.

Separately, a vehicle drove into a crowd at a Louisiana parade, injuring several people. Authorities have not yet confirmed a motive. FBI Director Kash Patel also issued a blunt public warning to anyone who attacks law enforcement, saying the bureau will "put you down" - language that drew praise from supporters of aggressive policing and concern from civil liberties advocates.

sources.title: DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE
Several injured after vehicle drives into crowd at Louisiana parade
FBI's Patel delivers blunt warning to law enforcement attackers: 'We're going to put you down'

us-politics.headline: Wisconsin GOP Struggles, China Tops US in Global Approval
us-politics.body:
Wisconsin Republicans are facing internal fractures highlighted by a recent state Supreme Court race, with the party struggling to consolidate around candidates and messaging in a key battleground state. The difficulties point to broader tensions within the GOP between its Trump-aligned base and more traditional conservative voters.

A new Gallup poll shows China has surpassed the United States in global approval ratings, a finding that cuts across partisan lines. The left points to Trump's trade and diplomatic posture as accelerating the decline. The right argues the metric reflects foreign media bias rather than genuine sentiment. Either way, the numbers hand critics a concrete data point at a moment when US standing abroad is already under debate.

sources.title: Wisconsin judicial race spotlights state GOP’s struggles
China now tops US in global approval ratings: Gallup

au-politics.headline: Australia Secures Fuel Supply Assurances as Price Crackdown Bites
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian partners have told Australia that fuel supplies will continue at normal levels, easing pressure on the Albanese government as it manages a domestic shortage. The diplomatic assurances come as the government moves to contain economic fallout from disrupted supply chains.

On the home front, regulators are hitting back hard. Nearly 100 NSW service stations face fines for displaying misleading petrol prices during the crunch, and motorists are being urged to report suspected price gouging through the FuelCheck platform. Western Australia is also expanding its FuelWatch scheme, requiring all regional stations to report prices from May.

The multi-front response signals the government is treating the shortage as both a foreign policy and consumer protection problem. Whether diplomatic assurances translate to stable prices at the bowser remains to be seen.

sources.title: Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
Nearly 100 NSW service stations face fines over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands
Motorists urged to dob in service stations over fuel price hikes

au-politics.headline: Murder Arrest at Melbourne Airport; Tasmania Police Shooting
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport while attempting to board an international flight, and has since been charged with murdering a woman. Police did not release details of the victim or the man's intended destination.

Separately, a man is in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police in the Hobart suburb of Goodwood. Circumstances surrounding the incident remain under investigation.

Also in Victoria, the sisters of police officer Neal Thompson - killed by Dezi Freeman - have announced they will sue Victoria Police. The civil action is expected to scrutinise the force's handling of events leading to Thompson's death.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police
Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: Queensland Road Toll Rises; Highway Reopens After Cyclone Narelle
au-politics.body:
Three people died and two remain in critical condition following a series of crashes on Queensland roads over the Easter weekend. Authorities have not yet released details on the locations or causes of each crash, but the toll adds to longstanding concerns about holiday road safety.

On a more positive note, the North West Coastal Highway has reopened after being closed by Cyclone Narelle, allowing Easter travellers to resume road trips through the region. The reopening was welcomed by locals and tourists alike who had faced significant detours.

sources.title: Three dead, two critical in spate of crashes on Queensland roads
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle

au-politics.headline: Victoria's First Nations Treaty Body Holds Historic First Elections
au-politics.body:
Victoria's First Nations treaty body is conducting its inaugural elections, a milestone in the state's formal treaty process with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Participants have framed the elections as an exercise in self-determination, with one community member quoted as saying 'we're the experts in our own lives.'

The treaty process has been years in the making and remains one of the most advanced of its kind in Australia. Critics have questioned the pace and structure of the process, while supporters argue it represents meaningful recognition of First Nations sovereignty. The outcome of these elections will shape the body's negotiating position with the Victorian government.

sources.title: ‘We’re the experts in our own lives’: Victoria’s First Nations treaty body is holding its first elections

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Escalates as Pilot Rescued
world.body:
US and Israeli forces have intensified strikes on Iranian infrastructure, hitting universities, bridges, and a Tehran psychiatric hospital. Trump confirmed the rescue of a downed US fighter pilot, saying simply "we got him" - the airman had been shot down over Iran in one of the conflict's most dramatic episodes so far.

Iran fired on a Kuwait oil complex and rejected a US ultimatum, while its coastline defences remain active. The US arrested relatives of slain general Qasem Soleimani, with Secretary of State Rubio personally stripping Soleimani's niece of her US residency. Satellite firm Planet Labs announced a blackout on imagery related to the war, raising concerns about information access during the conflict.

sources.title: Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images
Iran war live: Trump confirms rescue of US officer, says ‘we got him’
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
Tehran psychiatric hospital not usable after US-Israeli strike
Universities hit as US, Israel ramp up attacks on Iran’s infrastructure
Iran’s coastline: architecture of control
Marco Rubio says he has stripped Qassem Soleimani’s niece of US residency
New video reveals moment US-Israeli munitions impact Iranian bridge

world.headline: Iran War Ripples Across Region and Beyond
world.body:
The conflict is reshaping diplomacy and security across multiple continents. Italy's Meloni flew to Qatar to secure energy supplies as Iranian oil disruptions bite European markets. Pakistan faces mounting pressure over its proximity to the war zone, with analysts warning of economic and refugee spillover risks.

Indonesia received the bodies of UN peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon, a reminder of how regional instability is claiming lives beyond the main front lines. Germany separately announced that men under 45 may soon need military approval before taking extended trips abroad, reflecting a broader European shift toward wartime-footing governance.

sources.title: German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad
How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?
Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon
Italy’s Meloni meets Qatar emir to discuss energy issues amid Iran war

world.headline: Israel Cracks Down at Home and in Occupied Territories
world.body:
Israeli police arrested antiwar demonstrators in Tel Aviv as domestic opposition to the government's military campaigns grows louder. In the occupied West Bank, new Israeli restrictions have further tightened access to Hebron's Ibrahimi Mosque, a site sacred to both Muslims and Jews, continuing a pattern of incremental control over Palestinian religious life.

In Gaza, Israeli drone strikes continued, with footage showing a vehicle engulfed in flames in a central area of the territory.

sources.title: Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque
Israeli police arrest antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv
Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza

world.headline: Russia Kills Five at Ukrainian Market; Other World News
world.body:
A Russian strike on a Ukrainian market killed five people, the latest in a string of attacks on civilian infrastructure as the war there grinds on largely out of the global spotlight.

Elsewhere, a car ploughed into a Lao New Year parade in Louisiana, injuring around 15 people. A man was charged in New York over the fatal shooting of a baby in a pram. Cambodia unveiled a statue honouring Magawa, the landmine-sniffing rat credited with saving countless lives. And Barcelona's Robert Lewandowski scored a late winner at Atletico Madrid to keep his side's La Liga title push alive.

sources.title: Cambodia unveils statue to honour famous landmine-sniffing rat
Man charged over fatal shooting of baby in pram in New York
Car slams into Louisiana Lao New Year parade, injuring about 15 people
Lewandowski claims late La Liga win for Barcelona at Atletico
Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five

ai.headline: AI Clones Folk Artist, Then Files Copyright Against Her
ai.body:
A folk musician named Murphy Campbell found herself targeted by an AI system that copied her music and then turned copyright law against her - filing claims on content she originally created. The case is a sharp illustration of how AI tools can weaponize intellectual property frameworks against the very artists they exploit.

The story lands alongside a broader cultural anxiety about AI-generated work. A developer writing Lisp noted that the language is unusually resistant to AI assistance, finding the experience unexpectedly isolating. Meanwhile, The Verge reports a growing push to certify human-made creative work, as audiences and platforms struggle to distinguish authentic output from generated content. Provenance is becoming a product feature.

sources.title: AI that copied musical artist files copyright claim against that artist
Writing Lisp Is AI Resistant and I'm Sad
A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Anthropic's Claude Code Leak Spawns Malware Campaign
ai.body:
Hackers are distributing files claiming to be a leaked version of Anthropic's Claude Code tool, bundling them with malware. Wired reports the campaign is spreading across forums and file-sharing sites, targeting developers curious about the leak.

The timing is awkward for Anthropic. The company simultaneously announced that Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra to access OpenClaw, a move that drew immediate criticism from users already paying for the base product. Separately, researchers at Anthropic published findings on how emotion concepts function inside large language models - a quieter piece of work that risks being overshadowed by the week's security and pricing headlines.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Cuts PostgreSQL Performance in Half on AWS
ai.body:
An AWS engineer has reported that Linux 7.0 drops PostgreSQL performance by roughly 50 percent, and a fix may not come quickly. Phoronix details the regression, which appears tied to kernel-level changes affecting how the database handles I/O. The scale of the drop is striking - this is not a marginal slowdown.

The finding puts pressure on teams running PostgreSQL in cloud environments who are considering or have already adopted the new kernel. AWS has not yet issued formal guidance, and the upstream fix path looks complicated. For database-heavy workloads, the practical advice for now is to stay on an older kernel.

sources.title: AWS Engineer Reports PostgreSQL Perf Halved by Linux 7.0, Fix May Not Be Easy

ai.headline: Tech Giants Move to Gut Colorado's Right-to-Repair Law
ai.body:
Technology companies are lobbying hard to weaken Colorado's right-to-repair legislation, one of the strongest such laws in the United States. Ars Technica reports that industry groups are pushing amendments that would carve out broad exceptions, effectively neutralizing the law's core protections before it fully takes effect.

Colorado's law was designed to give consumers and independent repair shops genuine access to parts, tools, and documentation. The industry push mirrors tactics used in other states, where repair-friendly bills have been diluted through late-stage amendments. The outcome in Colorado is being watched closely - it could set the template for how right-to-repair fights play out nationally.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law

btc.headline: Bitcoin Faces Quantum Threat, Developers Race to Respond
btc.body:
With $1.3 trillion in assets at stake, Bitcoin developers are pushing hard on quantum-resistant cryptography before advances in quantum computing can threaten the network's security model. The urgency is real - sufficiently powerful quantum machines could theoretically break the elliptic curve cryptography underpinning Bitcoin wallets.

Jimmy Song is arguing from a different angle, calling for a more conservative node client to reduce protocol risk and slow the pace of changes that could introduce vulnerabilities. The two positions reflect a broader tension in Bitcoin development: how aggressively to evolve a system whose value depends on stability.

sources.title: Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain
Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client

btc.headline: Bitcoin Wins Political Converts as Macro Stress Mounts
btc.body:
Former UK Chancellor Philip Hammond has publicly backed Bitcoin as an alternative to financial systems he says are failing - a notable shift from a figure who spent years inside mainstream economic policymaking. His comments land as institutional interest in Bitcoin's crisis-performance credentials grows.

Research from Mercado Bitcoin adds fuel to that narrative, finding that Bitcoin has historically outperformed both gold and equities in the aftermath of major global shocks. The pattern suggests investors are increasingly treating Bitcoin as a recovery asset rather than just a speculative one. Political endorsements and data-backed performance claims are converging at an awkward moment for traditional markets.

sources.title: Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds
Ex-UK Chancellor backs bitcoin as alternative to failing systems

btc.headline: Iran's Telegram Ban Backfires, Durov Says
btc.body:
Telegram founder Pavel Durov says Iran's attempt to ban the platform drove users toward it rather than away, undermining the government's goal of controlling information flow. The claim fits a recurring pattern where heavy-handed internet restrictions generate publicity and workarounds that amplify the very content authorities want suppressed.

For the crypto world, Telegram remains a primary communications layer for projects, communities, and trading groups - making its resilience in restrictive markets directly relevant to how decentralized ecosystems operate under pressure.

sources.title: Telegram founder Pavel Durov says Iranian government's ban backfired

food.name: Mafaldine al Limone e Bottarga
food.description: Wavy ribbon pasta tossed in brown butter, lemon zest, and shaved cured mullet roe for a briny, citrus-bright hit of umami.
food.tip: Grate the bottarga over the pasta off the heat — direct heat kills its delicate oceanic flavour.

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that climbs through three effort zones — easy jog for 10 mins, steady tempo for 20 mins, then a hard 10-minute push — training your body to sustain pace under accumulating fatigue.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous systems of crayfish, and increasingly in medical devices for balance and hearing.
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and opens a rabbit hole connecting neuroscience, signal processing, and why your brain might function better with a little chaos in it.
